paper_title: NTL-amplified cryogenic light detectors with optically transparent electrodes

perturbations.why_wrong: The table values for ITO and Gold have been swapped. This contradicts the subsequent text in the 'ITO Deposition Procedure' section, which states the ITO working pressure was 9.6e-3 mbar and the rate was 28 nm/min, whereas the table now lists these under the Gold column.

perturbations.original:
ITO films were deposited by DC magnetron sputtering using a 4-inch balanced planar magnetron cathode equipped with a commercial 4N purity ITO target (90~wt.\% In$_2$O$_3$ -- 10~wt.\% SnO$_2$).
The working pressure during deposition was maintained at $9.6 \times 10^{-3}$~mbar with high-purity argon (99.999\%) supplied as sputtering gas at a constant flow rate of 17~sccm; no additional oxygen was introduced into the plasma.
All depositions were carried out at room temperature without intentional substrate heating.
Under these conditions, we measured an approximate deposition rate of 28~nm\,min$^{-1}$. This value is determined measuring ex-situ the thickness of a test deposition with a contact profilometer, and diving this quantity by the deposition time.
perturbations.perturbed:
ITO films were deposited by DC magnetron sputtering using a 4-inch balanced planar magnetron cathode equipped with a commercial 4N purity ITO target (90~wt.\% In$_2$O$_3$ -- 10~wt.\% SnO$_2$).
The working pressure during deposition was maintained at $8.8 \times 10^{-3}$~mbar with high-purity argon (99.999\%) supplied as sputtering gas at a constant flow rate of 16~sccm; no additional oxygen was introduced into the plasma.
All depositions were carried out at room temperature without intentional substrate heating.
Under these conditions, we measured an approximate deposition rate of 30~nm\,min$^{-1}$. This value is determined measuring ex-situ the thickness of a test deposition with a contact profilometer, and diving this quantity by the deposition time.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text now claims a pressure of 8.8e-3 mbar and a rate of 30 nm/min for ITO, which contradicts Table 1 where these specific values are assigned to the Gold bonding pad (ITO is listed as 9.6e-3 mbar and 28 nm/min in the original table).

perturbations.original:
Gold contact pads were deposited for device fabrication using a similar magnetron sputtering system equipped with a 2-inch gold target. The deposition was performed at a power of 40 W under an argon working pressure of $8.8 \times 10^{-3}$~mbar.
A gold thickness of 300 nm was deposited without the use of any adhesion layer (e.g., Ti or Cr). The absence of an intermediate layer was chosen to avoid potential interface diffusion or additional stress contributions that could affect cryogenic performance.
perturbations.perturbed:
Gold contact pads were deposited for device fabrication using a similar magnetron sputtering system equipped with a 2-inch gold target. The deposition was performed at a power of 100 W under an argon working pressure of $9.6 \times 10^{-3}$~mbar.
A gold thickness of 300 nm was deposited without the use of any adhesion layer (e.g., Ti or Cr). The absence of an intermediate layer was chosen to avoid potential interface diffusion or additional stress contributions that could affect cryogenic performance.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims a power of 100 W and pressure of 9.6e-3 mbar for Gold, which contradicts Table 1 where the Gold bonding pad parameters are explicitly listed as 40 W and 8.8e-3 mbar.

The section title is changed to 'Thermal characterization', but the content immediately following describes reflectance measurements, spectrophotometers, and optical thickness estimation, making the title factually inconsistent with the section's content.

perturbations.original:
For this production, the ITO thickness was targeted to absorb photons at the peak value of lithium molybdate emission (550-600 nm), the scintillator chosen for the CUPID experiment \cite{Cupid:2025avs}. 
To verify that the target was met, we performed reflectance measurements of the ITO coatings deposited on both sides of the silicon wafer.
These measurements were performed at room temperature, probing both the center of and the edge of the coating with a spectrophotometer (Konica Minolta and CM-2600d). By performing the measurements at two different positions, deposition non-uniformity associated with the magnetron sputtering source can be evaluated. From the visual inspection of the samples shown in Figure~\ref{fig:devices}, a difference is expected, since the samples show a radial color change.
perturbations.perturbed:
For this production, the ITO thickness was targeted to absorb photons at the peak value of lithium molybdate emission (550-600 nm), the scintillator chosen for the CUPID experiment \cite{Cupid:2025avs}. 
To verify that the target was met, we performed reflectance measurements of the ITO coatings deposited on both sides of the silicon wafer.
These measurements were performed at room temperature, probing both the center of and the edge of the coating with a spectrophotometer (Konica Minolta and CM-2600d). By performing the measurements at two different positions, deposition non-uniformity associated with the magnetron sputtering source can be evaluated. From the visual inspection of the samples shown in Figure~\ref{fig:devices}, no difference is expected, since the samples show a perfectly uniform color.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims no difference is expected and the color is uniform, which contradicts the caption of Figure 2 (S0015) and the subsequent text (S0018) which both state that variations are expected and visible diffraction patterns/radial color changes are present.

The caption now claims the electrodes were designed to 'maximize' reflection, but the very next sentence in the paper (S0017) states that ITO4 shows a 'minimum' in the 500-600 nm region, which is the intended behavior for an anti-reflective coating.

perturbations.original: The results of these measurements are reported in Figure~\ref{fig:reflectivity} for both ITO1 and ITO4 samples.
perturbations.perturbed: The results of these measurements are reported in Figure~\ref{fig:reflectivity} for both ITO2 and ITO3 samples.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text refers to samples ITO2 and ITO3, but the figure caption (S0015) and the following analysis (S0017) explicitly identify the samples as ITO1 and ITO4.

perturbations.original: ITO4 shows the expected reflectivity spectrum, with a minimum in the 500-600~nm region. On the other hand, ITO1 presents a reflectivity spectrum with two peaks typical of thick samples.
perturbations.perturbed: ITO1 shows the expected reflectivity spectrum, with a minimum in the 500-600~nm region. On the other hand, ITO4 presents a reflectivity spectrum with two peaks typical of thick samples.
perturbations.why_wrong: This swaps the characteristics of the two samples. The figure caption (S0015) states the objective was demonstrated for ITO4 (meaning it has the minimum), and the subsequent thickness calculation (S0019) uses the 'thick sample' formula specifically for ITO1.

perturbations.original: The depositions on the two sides of both silicon wafers have similar responses, as well as the corner and central regions. ITO1 shows higher variations, as expected from its appearance.
perturbations.perturbed: The depositions on the two sides of both silicon wafers have identical responses, showing no variation between the corner and central regions. ITO1 shows the highest uniformity, as expected from its appearance.
perturbations.why_wrong: This contradicts the figure caption (S0015) which states 'The comparison between sides and positions demonstrates some variation' and 'ITO1 shows higher variations'.

The formula in the equation has been changed to include a factor of 4 in the denominator instead of 2. This is mathematically inconsistent with the standard thin-film interference physics described in the context of determining thickness from adjacent maxima.
